text: Heat acclimation adaptations in end-exercise heart rate decay at a rate of approximately 2.3% per day of heat acclimation decay.
Heat acclimation adaptations in end-exercise core temperature decay at a rate of approximately 2.6% per day of heat acclimation decay.
Heat re-acclimation induces adaptations in heart rate and core temperature 8-12 times faster than the rate at which those adaptations are lost during decay.
A minimum of 5 days of heat acclimation is sufficient to cause meaningful adaptations in heart rate and core temperature.
Longer daily heat exposure duration during acclimation enhances the sustainability of core temperature adaptations during the decay period.
Sweat rate adaptations are better maintained when the initial heat acclimation period is longer, and are not significantly related to the number of decay days.
High heat exposure intensity during acclimation produces more sustained sweat rate adaptations, but lower intensity with longer duration is better for core temperature adaptations.
Heat re-acclimation provides a practical strategy to maintain heat adaptations during taper periods before competition, since re-acclimation is much faster than initial acclimation.
